alsoKnownAs: Red Devil Cichlid (trade overlap with A. labiatus)

nativeRange.habitat: Large lakes, rocky shorelines, and sandy littoral zones; found primarily in Lake Nicaragua, Lake Managua, and associated river systems. Inhabits shallow to mid-depth areas with hard substrate and scattered vegetation.

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rock formations for territory delineation
thick sandy substrate (10+ cm) for spawning pit excavation
minimal or no live plants (will be uprooted and destroyed)
PVC or ceramic caves as shelter and breeding sites
robust driftwood for additional territory markers

diet.notes: Opportunistic omnivore in the wild, consuming molluscs, crustaceans, insects, plant matter, algae, and detritus. In captivity, thrives on high-quality large cichlid pellets as a staple. Supplement with frozen foods (krill, shrimp, earthworms, bloodworm) and occasional vegetable matter (blanched zucchini, peas). Live feeder fish should be avoided — associated with disease transmission and nutritional imbalance. Feed once or twice daily; avoid overfeeding as the species is prone to obesity.

goodWith: bonded mate of the same species (established pair only)
similarly sized robust Central American cichlids in very large tanks (with caution)
large armoured catfish (common pleco, sailfin pleco) as dither/tank-mate in adequately sized setups
avoidWith: all small or medium-sized fish (will be killed or eaten)
other cichlids unless in extremely large tanks with territory breaks
fish with long flowing fins (will be attacked)
conspecifics outside a bonded pair (severe aggression)
any other Amphilophus or Vieja species (hybridisation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Amphilophus citrinellus; shows typical wild-type dark-barred juvenile/sub-adult coloration.
Clear lateral photograph of adult gold morph male with developed nuchal hump; well-lit, good resolution. Recommended candidate.
SeriouslyFish species profile image; good quality but copyrighted — use Wikimedia candidate instead.

summary: Iconic Central American cichlid reaching 35 cm; adult males develop a prominent cranial nuchal hump ('kok'). Comes in wild-type barred and striking gold/orange morphs. One of the parent species of the artificial Flowerhorn hybrid lineage. Fiercely territorial; best kept as a single specimen or bonded pair in a 500 L+ species tank.
careNotes:
The Midas Cichlid is one of the largest and most aggressive of the Neotropical cichlids. Adult males can attain 35 cm total length and develop a pronounced nuchal hump ('kok') that enlarges with age and condition — gold and orange color morphs in particular are prized in the hobby. A minimum tank volume of 500 L with at least 180 cm footprint is required for a single adult or a bonded pair. The tank should be heavily filtered with robust biological and mechanical filtration; Midas Cichlids are messy, high-bioload fish. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are advisable.

The species is highly territorial and destructive: all décor (rocks, PVC, wood) must be heavy and well-secured as substrate excavation and bulldozing is constant. Live plants are not suitable — use plastic or silk alternatives if greenery is desired. Sand substrate of at least 10 cm depth accommodates natural pit-digging during spawning and general foraging behaviour.

Midas Cichlids are typically kept as a single specimen or a single bonded pair. A paired male and female will defend their territory with extreme intensity, attacking anything that enters it, including the keeper's hand during water changes. Tankmates are generally not recommended; a few very large armoured catfish (Pterygoplichthys) may be tolerated in very large tanks but are still at risk during breeding periods.

The species readily hybridises with related Amphilophus and Vieja species; A. citrinellus crossed with A. trimaculatus is documented as one of the founding lineages of the artificially produced Flowerhorn (see fw-fish-348). Do not keep Midas Cichlids with other Amphilophus or Vieja species unless deliberately breeding hybrids is the intent.

The trade name 'Red Devil' is frequently misapplied to both A. citrinellus (Midas Cichlid) and A. labiatus (True Red Devil Cichlid). The Midas is typically rounder-bodied with a larger nuchal hump at maturity, while A. labiatus has pronounced fleshy lips. When purchasing, verify the species with the vendor where possible.
breedingNotes:
Substrate-spawning open brooder. A bonded pair will excavate a flat pit or select a flat rock surface as a spawning site and clean it meticulously before spawning. Females lay 1,000–2,000 adhesive eggs in typical clutches; males fertilise immediately after. Both parents share intensive brood care: eggs are fanned, unfertilised eggs removed, and newly hatched wrigglers are moved to pre-dug pits in the substrate. Both parents fiercely defend the fry against all intruders for 4–6 weeks until the juveniles are free-swimming and foraging independently.

Breeding is best achieved by introducing two juveniles and allowing them to pair naturally; forced pairing of adults frequently results in the death of one fish. A divider is sometimes used during initial introduction of adults. Breeding pairs often become more aggressive towards the keeper during spawning; heavy gloves are advisable during maintenance. Fry accept finely crushed cichlid pellets and micro-worms from free-swimming stage. Remove fry before parents begin preparation for the next spawn cycle or the juveniles may be attacked.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Amphilophus citrinellus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 30 cm, females smaller), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 5–20 dH, native range (Lake Nicaragua, Lake Managua, associated rivers in Nicaragua and Costa Rica), biotope, diet (omnivore), spawning behaviour (open substrate-spawner, biparental care), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Amphilophus citrinellus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum total length (35 cm TL males), native range (Nicaragua, Costa Rica), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), depth range, temperature (20–28°C), diet (omnivore — invertebrates, plant material). FishBase maximum size of 35 cm used per brief.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Midas Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide confirming 500 L+ tank requirement, high aggression, species-only recommended, diet (pellets, frozen, earthworms), and nuchal hump development in males. Noted gold color morph as the classic 'Midas' form.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Midas Cichlid
additional.notes: Supplemental source confirming habitat (rocky lakeside and sandy zones, Lake Nicaragua and Lake Managua), biparental substrate-spawning behaviour, colour polymorphism (dark wild-type vs gold morph), and Flowerhorn hybrid parentage. Aggression level and 500 L+ minimum tank size corroborated.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Central American Cichlids
additional.notes: TFH historical coverage of A. citrinellus; confirmed colour polymorphism genetics, nuchal hump ('kok') development and hormonal basis, relationship to Flowerhorn hybrids (A. citrinellus × A. trimaculatus lineage), and long captive lifespan (10–15 years documented in optimal conditions).

fish.breedingNotes: Open substrate-spawner; clutches of 1,000–2,000 eggs on flat rocks or excavated pits. Both parents provide intense biparental care for 4–6 weeks. Pair naturally from a group of juveniles; forced adult pairing risks fatality. Divider introductions recommended for adult pairings.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 35cm; aggressive Central American cichlid that grows fast and develops large cranial hump. Single-fish species-only tank typical. Requires 500L+. Hybridizes with related Vieja/Amphilophus species creating Flowerhorn lineage (fw-fish-348).
